title: The way of the Bull
subtitle: How a 3-ton bronze beast ended up in Lower Manhattan

steps.title: A workshop on Crosby Street
steps.comment: Di Modica built the bull in a cramped, semi-legal loft on Crosby Street. For almost two years, the sculpture was formed in clay, plaster and wax. The bronze was cast across the river at Bedi-Makky Art Foundry in Brooklyn, the family-run shop that had cast the Iwo Jima Memorial decades earlier.

steps.title: Bedi-Makky Art Foundry
steps.comment: Across the East River, in a Greenpoint foundry the Makky family had run since the 1940s, the bull came into being one section at a time. Using the French sand-casting technique Istvan Makky brought from Hungary, his sons cast roughly thirty bronze sections — head, horns, neck, ribs, haunches, hooves — each one poured into a sand mould reused since the Iwo Jima Memorial was cast in the same yard in 1954. The sections were welded together in the foundry yard under an overhead crane. This is where the bull was actually assembled — not at Crosby Street. And it was from this yard, on the night of 14/15 December 1989, that the truck rolled out toward Wall Street.

steps.title: Night raid on Wall Street
steps.comment: At about 1.30 am, Di Modica and a few friends drove the bull into the Financial District. He had spent the previous nights studying the security routine and knew the guard would be gone for 4.5 minutes. They left the bull under the Christmas tree on Broad Street, outside the NYSE. No commission. No permit. By morning, brokers, tourists and journalists had gathered around it, while Di Modica handed out leaflets nearby.

steps.title: Towed to a Queens impound
steps.comment: The bull lasted less than a day outside the exchange. By evening, the NYSE chairman had ordered it removed, and the NYPD had hauled it to an impound lot in Queens. Then the newspapers joined in. The New York Post ran “Bah, Humbug!” on its front page. A removal order became a citywide argument.

steps.title: A permanent home at Bowling Green
steps.imageCaption: Tourists at the bull, Bowling Green
steps.comment: Public outcry forced a compromise. The NYC Parks Department allowed the bull to return downtown, but not to the exchange. It went slightly south, to the small paved island at Bowling Green, beside the city’s oldest public park. The permit was temporary. It still is.
